sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A behavioral-science methods class collected data from 160 students on nightly sleep and perceived stress. A summary plot showed that students sleeping fewer hours tended to report higher stress. The correlation between sleep hours and stress was r = -0.52. The instructor emphasized that correlation describes association and direction but does not determine whether poor sleep causes stress, stress reduces sleep, or a third variable affects both.
questions.stem: What does the negative correlation indicate?

choices.D: Students with fewer sleep hours tended to report higher stress
choices.A: More sleep was associated with higher stress
choices.C: The correlation proves the direction of causation

explanations.D: Correct. A negative correlation means that as sleep increases, stress tends to decrease. Thus fewer sleep hours tend to go with higher stress.
explanations.A: The passage does not support more sleep was associated with higher stress; it points instead to students with fewer sleep hours tended to report higher stress.
explanations.B: The passage does not support sleep and stress were unrelated; it points instead to students with fewer sleep hours tended to report higher stress.
explanations.C: The passage does not support the correlation proves the direction of causation; it points instead to students with fewer sleep hours tended to report higher stress.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Correlation coefficient for nightly sleep hours and perceived stress.

explanations.A: Correct. The sign is negative and the magnitude is moderate, so r = -0.52 indicates a moderate negative linear relationship.
explanations.B: The passage does not support a perfect positive relationship; it points instead to a moderate negative linear association.
explanations.C: The passage does not support no association at all; it points instead to a moderate negative linear association.
explanations.D: The passage does not support a ph value; it points instead to a moderate negative linear association.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.C: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.C: Correct. Correlation cannot resolve whether sleep affects stress, stress affects sleep, or a third variable such as workload affects both.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.B: Randomly assign students to a sleep-extension intervention and compare later stress
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.B: Correct. Manipulating sleep-related behavior with a control group would test causal direction more directly than another correlation.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.A: Sleep and stress were moderately associated, but causal direction remains unresolved
choices.B: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.A: Correct. The r value supports association while the instructor’s warning limits causal interpretation.
explanations.B: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A research team created a 10-item social-anxiety scale for a student health center. Internal consistency was high, with Cronbach’s alpha = 0.89. Test-retest reliability over two weeks was r = 0.81. The new scale correlated strongly with an established social-anxiety inventory (r = 0.76) and weakly with a math-skills test (r = -0.08). Students who completed the scale right before a graded presentation scored higher than those completing it during a routine clinic visit.
questions.stem: Which statistic best indicates internal consistency?

explanations.D: Correct. Cronbach’s alpha measures how consistently items on a scale relate to one another. The value of 0.89 indicates strong internal consistency.
explanations.A: The passage does not support test-retest r = 0.81; it points instead to cronbach’s alpha = 0.89.
explanations.B: The passage does not support correlation with math skills = -0.08; it points instead to cronbach’s alpha = 0.89.
explanations.C: The passage does not support the number of clinic visits; it points instead to cronbach’s alpha = 0.89.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Reliability and validity coefficients for a new social-anxiety scale.

questions.stem: The strong correlation with an established inventory supports what?

explanations.C: Correct. Convergent validity is supported when a new measure correlates with an established measure of the same construct.
explanations.A: The passage does not support discriminant validity only; it points instead to convergent validity.
explanations.B: The passage does not support random assignment; it points instead to convergent validity.
explanations.D: The passage does not support the bystander effect; it points instead to convergent validity.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.B: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.C: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.B: Correct. Context effects remain possible because students scored higher before a graded presentation, suggesting state anxiety or setting may influence responses.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.C: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.D: Use the scale while standardizing or recording administration context
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.C: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.D: Correct. The scale has promising reliability and validity, but presentation timing shows context should be controlled or interpreted carefully.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.C: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.A: The scale has promising psychometric evidence, but administration context should be considered
choices.B: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.A: Correct. This conclusion integrates reliability, convergent/discriminant validity, and the observed setting effect.
explanations.B: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
